content:
9 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Andres Siimon on UnsplashA couple of months ago, I attended a UX Research and UX Design meetup in San Francisco , where the conversation on table turned toward AI-powered research. A few people in the room were excitedly sharing how they were already using synthetic users to test concepts faster and get early feedback without recruiting participants. Others had questions and concerns wondering how reliable these simulated users could really be and whether they could replace any part of traditional research.
Since that conversation, my brain started noticing the same terms appearing everywhere: synthetic users, AI personas, simulated users, digital twins.
At first, I thought they were different names for the same idea: An AI pretending to be a user. But i wasd confused and curious
But after reading papers from Kim & Lee, Stanford researchers, and marketing researchers exploring AI-generated participants, I realized something important that these terms are not describing the same thing.
The difference is not just the sophistication of the AI model. So the bigger question is:
What human information is this AI representation built on
Is it based only on demographics? A persona? Thousands of survey responses? Or a deep understanding of one individual’s experiences, motivations, and behaviors?
And thats what i learnt
The quality of an AI-generated user depends on the quality of the human data behind it.
Why are we creating AI users in the first place?
UX research has always been about understanding people.
We use:

• interviews to understand motivations

• surveys to identify patterns

• usability studies to observe behavior

• personas to communicate insights
But traditional research has constraints.
We cannot interview every possible user. Large-scale studies take time and money. Surveys often have missing data. Teams need ways to explore ideas earlier and faster.
This is where AI-generated users entered the conversation.
But the question became:
Can AI help us simulate, extend, or predict human behavior?
The answer is: yes, but the type of study and AI user matters.
A simple way to think about AI-generated users
The easiest way to understand these concepts is as a spectrum.
The more personal human information the AI has, the closer it gets to representing an individual.
LESS HUMAN CONTEXT
↓
AI Persona ( ← a described "type" of user)
↓
Synthetic User (← many generated users from demographics)
↓
Survey-Based AI Prediction (← learned from thousands of real answers)
↓
Interview-Based Digital Twin (← learned from one real person, in depth)
↓
MORE HUMAN CONTEXTLet’s break down what each one actually means.
1. AI Persona -“Imagine a user like this”
An AI persona usually starts with a human-written description.
Priya, 32, marketing manager in Mumbai, shops online, values convenience.
A researcher writes: “Create a persona of a 28-year-old woman Priya living in San Francisco who works in tech, uses skincare products, cares about sustainability, and shops online.”
The AI now has this profile. You ask: “How would Priyareact to a refillable shampoo bottle?”
The AI answers. But where did “Priya” come from?
Not from a real dataset. Not from a real participant. The researcher created the character. The AI fills in the missing details using patterns it learned during training.
How is it created?
A basic AI persona is created using a user profile demographics, behaviors, goals, and motivations. The AI uses this context plus patterns learned from data to generate a likely response.
It is not Priya’s real opinion. It is what someone like Priya might say.
Researcher assumptions
+
AI general knowledge
↓
AI PersonaWhen is it useful?
That makes it genuinely useful before research: for brainstorming, for generating hypotheses, for pressure-testing “what might a user like this worry about?” so you walk into real interviews with sharper questions.
The limitation
The persona is useful for: brainstorming, role-playing and exploring possible perspectives
But it is not statistically grounded.
Some companies use “AI persona” and “synthetic user” almost interchangeably.
2. Synthetic Users: “Create many possible users”
This is where things start becoming different from AI personas. An AI persona usually begins with a description of a user type. A synthetic user usually begins with data about a real population.
Instead of asking: “What would Priya think?” we ask: “What might people like Priya think?”
The goal is not to create one imaginary person. The goal is to create many artificial users that resemble a target population.
How is it created?
Imagine a company studying skincare customers.
They may have research data showing that their users differ based on:

• age

• location

• income

• shopping behavior

• interests

• product preferences

• purchase motivations
what can make it more rich is if you have interviews , customer reviews survey datasets as well
Instead of creating one persona, they ask AI: “Generate 50 users that represent this customer population.”
The AI might create:
Synthetic User 1 Priya
29-year-old woman in California.Discovers skincare through TikTok. Reads ingredient lists before purchasing.Prefers premium brands
Synthetic User 2 Sarah
42-year-old customer in Texas.Compares reviews before buying.Prioritizes value over brand loyalty
Synthetic User 3 Tisha
25-year-old student.Influenced by creators and social recommendations
These users are not real people. No individual named Sarah or Priya exists behind these profiles. But unlike a simple AI persona, they are not created only from a researcher’s imagination.
They are generated to reflect patterns found in a population.
Real User Data
+
AI modeling
↓
Synthetic UsersWhen is it useful?
The appeal is obvious. Recruiting participants is often the slowest part of research.
Useful for the same early-stage work as synthetic users with breadth: exploring a space, surfacing hypotheses, deciding what’s even worth researching before you spend real recruiting money.
Synthetic users can act as an early research partner. For example:
Before launching a new financial product, a team could generate synthetic users representing:

• young professionals

• retirees

• first-time investors

• financially cautious customers
The goal is not to make final decisions based on AI responses. The goal is to identify possibilities.
The limitation
The catch is that synthetic users are persuasive. They’ll hand you detailed stories and firm opinions, and it’s easy to mistake fluency for truth.
Here is the most important part to think about
A synthetic user can only be as good as: the data it was created from , the assumptions used to define the population and the AI model’s ability to capture real variation
Synthetic users often capture directional trends but struggle to capture: the exact magnitude of opinions , the diversity of responses, extreme viewpoints
Example:
Real users may have:

• 20% strongly dislike

• 40% neutral

• 40% like
Synthetic users may cluster closer around:

• 30% neutral

• 40% slightly like

• 30% slightly dislike
They can miss the variation that matters in UX.
3. Digital Twins: “What would this specific person do?”
A digital twin attempts to represent a specific individual.
The question changes: Synthetic user:“What might someone like this do?”
To
Digital twin: “What would this specific person do?”
How are digital twins created?
Researchers collect rich information about an individual:

• interviews

• experiences

• motivations

• preferences

• values

• decision-making patterns
The Stanford research explored this approach by conducting long AI-led interviews with more than 1,000 participants.
The AI representation was then tested: Could it predict how the real person would respond?
The key idea: The AI is not only learning what the person answered. It is learning the context behind those answers.
Lets imagine two people:
Both are: 35 years old, live in the same city, have similar income
A demographic model might consider them similar. But their decisions may be completely different because of:

• family situation

• previous experiences

• motivations

• personal values
Demographics tell us: Who someone is. Context helps us understand: Why someone behaves the way they do.
This is why richer human context often improves AI representations.
Why are they useful?
The potential value of digital twins is scale. Researchers cannot realistically interview the person repeatedly if they are time bound and often stakeholders wants to move ahead quickly (Sometimes based on false assumptions backed on no actual research)
But if an AI representation can accurately capture aspects of an individual, researchers may be able to explore questions without repeatedly asking the person.
For example:
A researcher might ask: “How would Sarah react to this new product concept?”
or “Would Sarah find this feature valuable?”
This could help researchers explore ideas faster because they already have related rich contextual information about Sarah
What are the limitations?
Digital twins depend completely on the quality of the information used to create them.
A short description or a 10–15 min interview of a person will not capture the complexity of a human being. And then even a long interview captures only what someone shares and remembers.
People change. People contradict themselves.
People behave differently in different contexts.
A digital twin is not the person. It is still a model attempting to represent that person.
4. Survey-Based AI Models: Predicting what a person would answer
This is where the terminology becomes confusing.
When we hear “digital twin,” we imagine an AI model that deeply understands a specific person their personality, experiences, and motivations.
But some research uses the term differently. The work by Kim and Lee is an example.
Their goal was not to interview people and build a rich representation of their lives.
Instead, they explored whether AI could predict missing survey responses based on patterns learned from existing survey data.
How are these models created?
The researchers used a large survey dataset containing thousands of responses from many individuals. The AI analyzed relationships between answers.
For example:
Imagine thousands of people answering questions about:

• political beliefs

• social attitudes

• behaviors

• demographics
The model learns patterns such as:
“People who answered these questions in a certain way often answered another question similarly.”
Then the researchers removed some answers and asked the AI to predict what the person would have answered.
The evaluation was simple:
Did the AI prediction match the actual human response?
If the real person answered: “Agree”
and the AI predicted: “Agree”
the prediction was counted as correct.
Why is this useful?
This type of AI model could help solve problems researchers face with large surveys.
For example: A participant completes 80% of a survey but skips the rest. Instead of losing that data, AI could estimate possible responses. It could also help researchers analyze older datasets where certain questions were not asked at the time.
For example:
A survey conducted ten years ago may not have included a question researchers care about today.
AI could attempt to estimate how those participants might have responded.
What are the limitations?
The biggest limitation is that the model learns patterns from existing data. It is good at predicting within the world it has already seen. But it may struggle with completely new questions or situations.
Why?
Because predicting a missing answer is different from understanding a person’s deeper reasoning.
The model may know:
“People who answered these questions usually answer this way.”
But it may not know:
“Why does this person personally think this way?”
This distinction becomes important when we move from prediction to understanding human b
